It can also overclock lower voltage recipes, such as those in the Mk1 or Mk2 reactor. See the Mk1's quest for how these mechanics work.

quests.title: UHV Superconductors
quests.subtitle: Is Neutronate even a real thing?
quests.desc:
You probably don't even need these, but UHV Superconductors exist, and are used for a few things, which you also probably don't need...

Craft these if you feel like flexing.

quests.title: Highly Advanced SoCs
quests.subtitle: Can we get much higher?
quests.desc:
Highly Advanced SoCs will make crafting wetware circuits much easier.

The drawback is that the recipe requires UV power, but that's no problem to you, right?

quests.title: Steam Power
quests.subtitle: https://store.steampowered.com/
quests.desc:
You might already be familiar with EU as the Energy Unit for Electric machines - but what about Steam?

Steam machines will run using a proportional amount of steam at a 1:1 ratio of EU to Steam as listed in your recipe viewer of choice.

Regular Steam Machines are twice as slow to complete recipes as is listed in emi. To bring your machines up to speed, you may want to pursue High Pressure Steam machinery.

High Pressure Steam Machines are the same speed as indicated by emi, but with adjusted steam consumption. You will unlock them once you obtain Steel.

quests.title: Electricity
quests.desc:
You have likely realised by now that the Energy system in GregTech is nowhere near as simple as the RF you may be used to using in modded Minecraft.

The energy unit of GregTech is the EU.

EU mechanics have a certain flair to them, and are not dissimilar to real-world electricity. They can also be dangerous and punishing if mishandled. However, the power system gives GregTech players a lot of satisfaction when harnessed well.

Lore: The Amperage bug, exploit or feature (call it whatever you want!) from 1.12.2 GTCE has been fixed in GTCEu. The Energy network now works as intended.

Let's go over some basic physics:

° Power = Voltage * Amperage
° Energy = Power * Time

Resistance is an excluded concept in GregTech, instead being partially simulated as cable loss.

The units are defined as such:

- EU is the unit of Energy.
- EU/t is the Power.
- A or Amp is the Amperage.
- V would be the Voltage, but we prefer referring to it as LV, MV, HV... as those are the relevant values for gameplay (technically: LV = 32V = 32EU/t/A).

Electricity in GregTech travels in "packets". The size of each packet is equivalent to the voltage of its tier.

Taking an LV packet as an example, this "packet" would carry 32EU. Multiple blocks are capable of producing these EU packets, and packets are only produced if they are able to access a destination.

Every tick, EU packets attempt to transfer themselves from Energy Producers to Consumers. These packets have two parameters: a Voltage tier determined by the tier of the Producer, and Amperage. This determines how much Power can actually be transferred. 

There are no partial Amps, meaning Energy is transferred only if the full Amp packet can be received. You might notice this from your machines sitting just below full EU capacity - they will only accept EU when they are missing at least 1A of their input voltage.

If a Machine receives a higher Voltage than its Tier, it'll explode. Kaboom! Any excess Amperage won't have an adverse affect, as machines only consume as they need.

The machine explosion would usually also damage terrain, but we have disabled that feature in this pack.

In summary, Energy is transported in packets of 32EU for LV, 128EU for MV... so on and so forth. The amount of energy that is actually received by a machine can end up being lower than energy sent, due to cable loss.

Machine Energy Input is limited by Voltage (too high and, well, boom!), and by Amperage. Thus, a MV Machine with 2A Input connected to a LV line will never accept more than 64EU/t.

It is still possible to run MV Machines on LV lines, but only if the recipes are low enough Power, or if you have a MV Battery in the charging slot. The machine will only accept 1A when idle, and so the Battery will recharge at a maximum rate of 32EU/t.

quests.title: Quest
quests.desc:
Transporting EU is done via direct connection, using Wires or Cables.

Wires are the inferior option as they will electrocute you and lose more energy as the electricity travels through the wire, so you want to use insulated Cables. Keep in mind that you can't combine cables into thicker variants, so make sure you insulate the right thickness to avoid any headaches.

Cable loss means you will have to stick your Machines close to your Generators, but that will only be a problem in earlygame. If you peek through the quest book, you will eventually be able to obtain Superconductors, sidestepping this issue!

Keep in mind that GregTech cables suffer from "cable loss". Every block of cable that an EU packet travels will decrease its stored EU.

For instance, transferring LV current along 6 cables each with a loss of 5 EU/t will result in the packets arriving at their destination with a meagre 2 EU/t (32 - [6*5]). For this reason, you will want to keep your cables short until you can start to consider better alternatives.

Superconductors are special Wires available for each Voltage tier. They don't have any Cable counterpart, but that isn't necessary, as they have absolutely zero cable loss! They also don't damage you when you touch them. 

They are the best choice for wiring at any tier, if not a little expensive in comparison to the other wires.

GT6-style Cables are enabled. It means that placing Cables will only connect them to the block they were placed against. To open more connections, use your Wire Cutter on the grid.

GTCEu Cables now have native RF support! You can plug any RF appliance into an EU source and it will automatically convert. The ratio is 4 : 1 (RF : EU). Not that you can produce RF in this pack - but it's a thought if you wanted to make your own!

quests.title: Quest
quests.desc:
The quantity of 1 packet per tick is known as an Amp (1A for short).

Each single-block Generator in GregTech is configured to produce 1A of its voltage tier. For LV, that results in 32EU/t.

If a machine requires 16EU/t, one "packet" of 32EU is sent every other tick (assuming no cable loss) - no more, no less.

A Battery Buffer with 4 Batteries can produce up to 4 Amps for 128EU/t as LV power, which is important for the Electric Blast Furnace - or to run multiple machines simultaneously.

Generic machines are configured to draw up to 2 Amps to account for cable loss. Other machines have specific Amps IN, which is described in their tooltip.

The most critical thing to know is that cables have an Amperage limit. Attempting to transport more Amps than this limit will burn your cable up.

Amps are drawn only through the Cables connecting the Producers and Consumers directly, and only drawn if the Consumers are active.

This means you can safely step down your Cables in certain places - feel free to experiment!

quests.title: Quest
quests.desc:
Why does every machine have a million variants across each tier? We'll let you in on an industry secret - overclocking is one of the main reasons.

Overclocking is a mechanic that happens when you try to run a recipe in a machine that is higher tier than the recipe itself. The recipe will use an increased amount of energy, but the recipe time will be cut based on how many tiers you're overclocking by.

Lore: GTCE overclocking was extremely awkward. If the recipe consumed 16EU/t or below, it would do a classic 2.0x overclock. However if the recipe consumed more than 16EU/t, every Overclock would be 2.8x faster! GTCEu has a config to select the Overclock speed bonus.

A pro tip - for automated setups, overclock as little as possible!

Machines have a side menu to set the maximum voltage tier it will overclock to. Click on it to cycle through all the available Voltages and choose a limit.

Each tier above the recipe tier allows one overclock. Each overclock implies:

- 2x recipe speed
- 4x power consumption
-> 2x total energy cost

As you can see, there are upsides and downsides. It is a game of balance: ask yourself if you want faster recipes at the cost of greater power consumption.

quests.title: Multiblock University
quests.subtitle: Multiblock University
quests.desc:
There are two really important factors to know when building Multiblocks.

Check emi, and remember to keep things versatile.

Rule number one - check emi!

This one was probably obvious, but TMI is so much more than just a cute picture of how the Multiblock looks when built! Use your mouse to rotate the preview, and hover over the blocks used to view any extra information.

The TMI page also contains an ingredient list of required blocks. You can also right-click a block in the preview to get a display of all blocks that work in that specific position.

Rule number two - multiblocks are versatile!

There is no "definite" way to form the Multiblock. Most of the time, you have complete freedom over the tier of Buses/Hatches used - and you also have the ability to choose where you want your inputs/outputs.

Taking the EBF as an example, any tier of Energy/Bus/Hatch can be used, and they can be placed anywhere in place of a casing. Fluid Hatches are also optional, though you probably want them if you need to handle recipes involving fluid.

quests.title: Troubleshooting Multiblocks
quests.subtitle: It's not forming? We've all been there before.
quests.desc:
- If the recipe isn't happening, check if the Multiblock is properly plugged in (we all make these mistakes!).

- Remember to always perform the initial Maintenance "routine" at the appropriate hatch.

- Verify the recipe's EU/t usage - the max EU/t provided to the multiblock has to be greater than that of the recipe.

- Check the special requirements the recipe may have (TMI always has some kind of indication if this is the case). Do you need to be in a Cleanroom, for example?

- Make sure the correct items are input into the buses/hatches. This is less obvious than in a regular machine, because you have to juggle between the Input Bus and Input Hatch.

- Make sure there is space for an output. Maybe the multiblock formed, but you forgot a Hatch. The other biggest offender is the Output Hatches being too low tier and thus having too little capacity - this trips people up with the Greenhouse!

quests.title: Maintenance Hatches
quests.desc:
Maintenance in GregTech is not as bad as you think (promise!), and adding a Maintenance Hatch to your multiblocks makes them look pretty industrial!

Most Multiblocks require a Maintenance Hatch. You will need to perform Maintenance for the Multiblock to begin operating.

This is done by having a Wrench, a Screwdriver, a Soft Mallet, a Hammer, a Wire Cutter, and a Crowbar in your inventory, opening the Maintenance Hatch and clicking the center spot once. There's no need to move tools individually.

Alternatively, you can circumvent these problems by placing a Tape in the Maintenance Hatch, but a lot of people would jump at the chance to tell you that Tape is "unethical" - plus, tape is gated behind some polymers starting from MV.

Maintenance problems have a 5% chance of occuring every hour of multiblock activity. Needless to say, they are very rare. Each maintenance problem increases the recipe duration by 10%. Fixing these problems is done the same way as above.

At HV, you'll unlock other Maintenance Hatches that do not require manual repairs.

quests.title: Quest
quests.desc:
The Electric Blast Furnace, Pyrolyse Oven, Multi Smelter, and Cracking Unit all require Heating Coils. But why should you bother upgrading to higher tiers of Coil?

Coil progression can be viewed in the Milestones tab.

For the EBF, things get slightly complicated. The EBF smelts items at a certain temperature. This is mainly determined by the tier of heating coil used - Cupronickel smelts at 1800K, Kanthal at 2700K, etc. Additionally, starting from HV, each tier of energy adds 100K to the temperature.

How does this affect you? Each EBF recipe has a certain base temperature. For example, Aluminium is smelted at 1700K. If the EBF temperature is not high enough, the recipe won't happen. If the EBF temperature is high enough, 2 different bonuses are applied:

- For every 900K above the recipe temperature, the amount of energy required is multiplied by 0.95x - which stacks.
- For every 1800K above the recipe temperature, a single additional overclock will be 100% efficient; in other words, it will quadruple the recipe speed, instead of doubling it.

The Pyrolyse Oven is much more simple in comparison to the EBF.

Cupronickel coils apply a +25% penalty to recipe duration - effectively, recipes are slower than listed if one uses Cupronickel coils.

Kanthal coils restore recipe duration to a regular length of time.

However, coils above Kanthal apply an additive -50% bonus to recipe duration!

The Cracker is much more simple.

Every coil tier post-Cupronickel will reduce its energy usage by 10%.

Coils in the Multi Smelter reduce the amount of energy per operation whilst also increasing the number of items that can be processed in parallel.

quests.title: Muffler Hatches
quests.desc:
The Electric Blast Furnace requires a Muffler Hatch to run. This hatch must be unobstructed so it can output its beautiful smoke particles.

Lore: In GregTech 5, polluting multiblocks had Muffler Hatches. The higher tier the hatch, the lower the Pollution output.

Pollution had... interesting... effects, such as turning grass and dirt into sand.

When a recipe is performed, there is a small chance for the Muffler Hatch to give bonus items - typically tiny Dusts of Ash. These items can be processed for some helpful resources.

Do not try to automate the extraction of items from the Muffler Hatch! It's not intended to be an automation challenge, as it voids excess contents when full, so don't worry about it stopping machines from running.

Muffler Hatches in GTCEu are more about pure aesthetic (c'mon, you can't deny that those particles look good!) and small rewards, rather than punishing game mechanics.

quests.title: Powering Multiblocks
quests.desc:
Multiblocks do not look at the voltage of recipes when trying to craft. Instead, they simply check if they are being provided with enough Power.

Note: Be wary of Cable losses bringing the actual power input below the recipe requirement! We highly recommend either placing your Battery Buffer next to the Energy Hatches for the minimum cable length, or looking around this questbook to see if there are better alternatives to Cables.

The EBF needs a minimum of 120EU/t power input to run its recipes. This can be achieved with two LV Energy Hatches. Each one takes 2 Amps of LV, so that's 4 Amps of LV for 128EU/t maximum power.

quests.title: Multiblock I/O
quests.desc:
Similarly to the Steam Multiblocks - hopefully, you made these! - you're going to need some Buses if you want to move items in and out.

For fluids, you'll instead need Hatches. Higher tiers of Hatches/Buses have more slots.

Tip: You can Sneak + Right Click a placed Hatch or Bus while holding a Screwdriver (and an empty offhand) to swap its I/O Direction

quests.title: All About the Cleanroom
quests.desc:
First off, be sure to check the tooltip - there's a lot of information in there!

Don't forget the Maintenance Hatch. If the structure refuses to form, rotate or replace the Controller, it can be a bit finicky. Don't worry - it doesn't have to be cube-shaped.

The Controller always has to be in the center. The rest of the top layer has to be Filter Casings.

All edges have to be Plascrete, and the sides can be Cleanroom Glass.

Placing Generators or Mufflers inside is not allowed. They're too dirty, so the Cleanroom will unform.

Upon assembly, the Cleanroom starts contaminated. While contamined, contained machines will not be able to operate.

The Cleanroom will drain 30 EU/t and slowly increase the cleanliness until its cleanliness is at 100%.

Once Clean, recipes can run. At that point it will only draw 4EU/t. Keep it powered at all times or it will get dirty again!

The bigger the Cleanroom, the longer it takes to become Clean.

Note: When the time comes, Multiblocks must also be put into Cleanrooms in order to run cleanroom recipes!

Need to transfer EU? Try using Hulls or Diodes.

Item and Fluid transfer can be done using Passthrough Hatches.

Machine Hulls can also "double" as ME Cables to extend your ME Network inside your Cleanroom.

If you used any Door(s) in the structure, they can remain open. Not very realistic, but convenient!

Configs: If you dislike the Cleanroom mechanics, it can be toggled off in the config file with "B:enableCleanroom". Multiblocks can be made to ignore cleanroom requirements with "B:cleanMultiblocks". We encourage you to try it out before deciding.

In the real world, a Cleanroom is a well-isolated room which maintains a very low concentration of dust and airborne particles. They're normally used for scientific research, and in real-life semiconductor manufacturing. Clean rooms are specifically designed to keep out as many airborne particulates as can be. 

Clean rooms are also sometimes used with the purpose of preventing materials from escaping! This is often the primary aim in fields such as pharmaceutics, nuclear work, and hazardous biology.

The more you know!

quests.title: Understanding the Assembly Line
quests.desc:
There are many ways to automate Ordered Items: GT item pipes, Redstone, other mods, you name it! This quest will attempt to guide you through a setup using AE2.

Have the Pattern Provider face an Item P2P. Set two output P2Ps: one into the first Input Bus, and the other into Item Pipes. Make your Item Pipes connect to all Input Buses including the first. Don't forget Blocking Mode! 

With this setup, assembly items should be fully automated!

Whilst item automation was relatively simple, fluid automation is slightly harder.

Fluid Pipes behave differently compared to Item Pipes, so you won't be able to use the same solution as above. 

This quest won't explain how to do this entirely (due to it being off by default), so the full solution is left as an exercise to the reader.

quests.title: Implemented At Last
quests.subtitle: Implemented At Last
quests.desc:
Scanning is the first step for research. Take the item shown in TMI and put it with a Data Stick to scan it. This will consume the item and put Data on the Data Stick. Pretty straightforward, right?

You will need at least an HV Scanner to progress to LuV, an EV Scanner for LuV Energy Hatches, and an IV Scanner for Fusion Reactors.

Later on, recipes will require different Data Storage Items for research, such as the Data Orb. This process will remain the same, but will use different Data Storage Items instead. They can also be copied the same way, but you cannot copy between different types of storage items.

Eventually, you'll want multiple Assembly Lines, but you won't want to re-scan for every Research. This is why you can also use the Scanner to Copy Data. Place the data stick you want to copy in one slot, and a blank one in the other. This will copy the contents of the first stick into the second!

Important: Using two different data sticks with data on them will cause one to overwrite the other. Be careful!

quests.title: Quest
quests.desc:
Rotors have a Power, Efficiency and Durability that are based on tool stats.

Power is a multiplier applied to the production of the Turbine. This stacks multiplicatively with the Rotor Holder.

Efficiency reduces its fuel consumption. This again stacks multiplicatively with the Rotor Holder.

Durability is the lifetime of the Rotor in seconds, when the Turbine is active.

Rotor Holders above the tier of the Large Turbine will multiplicatively double the production for every tier, whilst additively increasing the efficiency by 10% every tier.

Some pros of Large Turbines:

- Turbines can scale Power production far, far higher than their base output, thanks to tiered Rotor Holders.

- The Rotor can give a huge boost to Fuel efficiency and Power production.

- You get a lot of satisfaction for setting them up properly (yay!)

...and some cons of Large Turbines:

- Turbines work off Inertia, which requires them to continuously run to achieve maximum Power.

- Any excess Energy produced will be voided. You will need to use large Energy Buffers and an Advanced Energy Detector if you wish to stay efficient.

- They require a Rotor, which will lose durability over time and eventually break.

- A little bit of Math is required to get the expected results.

A Turbine's Power output affects both the fuel consumption and the EU/t generation. It is calculated with the following formula:

production = baseEUt x rotorPower x 2^(rotorHolderTier - turbineTier)

The Efficiency reduces fuel consumption, but does not alter the EU/t generation. It is calculated with the following formula:

efficiency = rotorEfficiency x (1 + 0.1 x (rotorHolderTier - turbineTier))

Let's simulate a Large Steam Turbine, with an Ultimet Rotor and an EV Rotor Holder:

Production = 1024 x 1.60 x 2 = 3276 EU/t

Efficiency = 1.30 x 1.10 = 143%

(Therefore, steam consumption would be 4582 L/t - this is above a Titanium Boiler, or a Tungstensteel Boiler with ~70% throttle)

Next, we'll analyse the Gas Turbine, with a Chrome Rotor and an EV Rotor Holder:

Production = 4096 x 1.70 x 1 = 5325 EU/t

Efficiency = 1.55 x 1.00 = 155%

(The same fuel consumption would produce 3435 EU/t using 6.7 Turbo Gas Turbines!)

Finally, how about we check out the Plasma Turbine, with a HSS-S Rotor and a ZPM Rotor Holder:

Production = 16834 x 2.50 x 4 = 168340 EU/t

Efficiency = 1.80 x 1.20 = 216%

(This is more than a ZPM Amp!)

quests.title: Quest
quests.desc:
Simply put, Gregicality Multiblocks are larger versions of the GregTech machines you might be familiar with. The obvious increase in resource cost is worth your time, as they can be upgraded to gain the ability to process recipes in parallel.

They also introduce Machine Modes - depending on the configured machine mode, the Multiblock will use a different recipe type when processing materials. You can change this from the machine GUI by clicking on the Robot Arm button.

By including Parallel Control Hatches in your Gregicality Multiblocks, you can grant them parallel processing capability. There are 4 tiers of hatch, each further increasing parallel capability by an factor of 4.

Batch mode can be enabled on a multiblock to merge together an amount of one recipe together into one recipe up to 5 seconds. This does not change the overall speed of one recipe like overclocking, but is useful to stop machine flickering.
This is not strictly gated by Gregicality Multiblocks, but is extremely useful with them.

quests.title: Parallelisation
quests.desc:
Why go parallel?

- Parallelization is superior to overclocking! Take advantage of this for your passive setups such as Ore Processing or Oil Distilling.

- Distinct buses: GCYM Machinery can accept multiple Input Buses. Use it for your pattern automation with Extruder, Engraver, and many other.

Power cost is based on the current recipe being run, times the number of parallel being performed.

quests.title: Beginner Ore Processing
quests.desc:
Why process ore?

The main purpose of GregTech Ore Processing is to provide byproducts on top of the initial outcome. At Steam Age, none of the byproducts are obtainable, but this will become relevant soon.

How should I start?

The steps available in the Steam Age go as follows:

One Ore (block) -> Two Crushed Ores (Macerator)

One Crushed Ore -> One Impure Dust (Hammer)

One Impure Dust -> One Dust (In world crafting)

One Dust -> One Ingot (Furnace, you should know this one)

A pickaxe or mining hammer enchanted with Hammering, or a GregTech Hammer directly, gives Crushed Ores from breaking Ore blocks, but only at a 1:1 ratio.

Silk Touch is essential to obtain an ore block - this pack includes a recipe for the book.

The rule bends for ores giving more than two dusts (such as Redstone), and for dusts that cannot be smelted (Redstone... again!).

However, you can still smelt the ore directly to get half the amount you would from the full processing.

TMI does not indicate Impure Dust to Clean Dust conversion, so keep that in mind that you can throw them into filled cauldrons.

Take this reward for listening to us waffle on WITHOUT nodding off!

quests.title: Extracting an ore's full potential... at last!
quests.subtitle: Extracting an ore's full potential... at last!
quests.desc:
A shopping list for anyone looking to automate Ore Processing on a large scale:

- One or more IV Macerators.
- The Large Centrifuge. Set it to work as a Thermal Centrifuge, and you're off to the races.
- One or more MV Electrolyzers - you could possibly make the large version if there's enough demand.
- A Multi Smelter.
- A Packager of any tier for those pesky Small/Tiny Dusts (think Rare Earth!)
- Inventories acting as a large buffer, such as Tungstensteel Crates.
- ME Storage Buses, or a lot of Item Pipes + Filter covers, or Item Tag Filters.

What you may also want to add:

- A few LV Ore Washers for quick washing purposes.
- Alternately, a Large Ore Washer, accompanied with a set of Distilleries for Distilled Water.
- A second Large Centrifuge... this time set to Centrifuge mode for impure/purified dusts.
- Chemical Baths for the special Byproducts that require Mercury or Sodium Persulfate to obtain.
- Sifters of any tier for gem ores.
- Electromagnetic Separators.

If the Large Machines aren't your thing, you could always try using tons of LV machinery, and route with some clever piping.

Overclocking is not energy efficient, which is why it is generally better to go for low-tier machines in passive setups. One could also argue they are cheaper.

In previous versions of GregTech, you could just use Processing Arrays, as they could condense many low-tier machines down into a single speedy multiblock. Players desiring similar processing speeds nowadays should really look to the GCYM machinery - it's quick, easy to upgrade when you tier up (Energy Hatches are usually cheaper than 16 low-tier machines!), and c'mon now... they just look really cool.

"Should I go for the optional byproducts?"

Yes.

However, the "routes" you take your ores through processing is up to you. Want to macerate twice because you prefer the other byproducts? Go ahead!

Note that Thermal Centrifuging is usually a good idea, as doing so unlocks a unique Byproduct from the maceration step.

Chalcopyrite, Chalcocite, Bornite, Tetrahedrite, Pentlandite and Sheldonite ores should all be turned into their Purified Crushed variants and sent to your Platinum Group Metal chain.

Purified Crushed Galena/Sphalerite should be reserved for Indium until you set up the Naquadah line. At that point, you'll have more Indium than you'll ever need.

Gallium, Chrome, Niobium, Rare Earths, and Glowstone are all Byproducts you might want to prioritise. 

If you think we're missing any pertinent products, hit us up on Discord, and we'll be sure to add your favourites to this list.

As you've probably realised, Applied Energistics 2 will be very, very helpful when automating all of this.

ME Storage Buses are very powerful for routing items when they enter your ME network. They also have a huge whitelist or blacklist configuration. Attach them to some Tungstensteel Crates to buffer intermediate items, or use a dedicated AE2 subnet.

To combine Small Dusts into full Dusts, you'll want a Storage Bus on high priority paired with an Item Tag filter set to dusts/small. Note that Small Dusts may be used for Boules, and a few Tiny Dusts are needed for the PBI chain, so you can blacklist them from the filter if you desire.

For ores that you want to process quickly but don't care for the Byproducts as the full "route" would take too long in the machines, prioritize filtering them for quick washing.

The typically "best" path is: Macerator -> Ore Washing -> Thermal Centrifuge -> Macerator. If you deviate from it for various reasons, you're gonna have to set filters manually. Gasp!

quests.title: Colored Buses
quests.subtitle: Color by number multiblocks
quests.desc: Spraying Input Buses and Hatches the same color will treat them as one Hatch in the Multiblock. With distinct mode enabled on the Buses/Hatches, the Multiblock will only search for a recipe using ingredients from one color group. With this enabled the Multiblock will try to match the color of the Output Hatch to the color group used for the inputs.

quests.title: Promoting a Sustainable Playthrough
quests.subtitle: Very conscious of you!
quests.desc:
You may be wondering how to obtain large quantities of common resources used in GregTech. This might be old news to seasoned players, but it's actually possible to set up passive processes to make various materials and chemicals fully renewable.

We've added this chapter to lead you towards some setups that you might desire. Obviously, there's no need to do all of these, but who doesn't love "free" resources.

A footnote: There's no way we could document all of the renewable routes in GregTech. This quest page merely serves as a primer to lead newer players through their options. Not every option listed in this quest book is optimal, so we urge you to experiment!

quests.title: More like game breaking, am I right?
quests.subtitle: More like game breaking, am I right?
quests.desc:
Ah, the Rock Crusher - such an unassuming machine that initially appears to only really be a builder's best friend. Who could look past infinite Cobblestone, Deepslate, or Obsidian?

Don't be fooled, though, as this little machine might just shock you with the array of materials it makes renewable when you macerate its rocks down to dust form. The surrounding quests will clue you in.

quests.title: The Joy of Cobblestone
quests.subtitle: Oh, we're only just getting started.
quests.desc: Cobblestone is just the start of things. Let's make a Cobbleworks!

quests.title: Stone Dust
quests.subtitle: Small Stone, Big Byproducts
quests.desc:
Stone Dust can be centrifuged in MV for a wide range of miscellaneous materials. It's a slow and expensive process, but you may wish to check it out.

Obviously, Quartzite is Quartzite. Did you know that you could extract any excess Quartzite into Liquid Glass?

Potassium Feldspar electrolyses into some incredibly useful resources.

You also have a (low) chance for Biotite, which notably electrolyses for a very slow source of Fluorine.

Finally, Metal Mixture dust is useless until you can centrifuge it at EV. Pretty much all of its outputs when centrifuged are very useful.

quests.title: Gravel
quests.subtitle: cp_gravelpit
quests.desc:
Just Forge Hammer that Cobblestone and you'll get some Gravel.

Gravel macerates into Stone Dust, but with a low chance of Flint when the recipe is performed in a HV Macerator or above. You probably want to sift for Flint, anyway!

Flint Dust centrifuges into Silicon Dioxide, which is then electrolysed for Silicon and Oxygen.

quests.title: Sand
quests.subtitle: Two vast and trunkless legs of stone...
quests.desc:
Yet another Forge Hammer step, but you'll be making Sand renewably. With renewable Sand comes renewable Glass (remember to try using an Arc Furnace for that sweet doubled yield!).

Quartz Sand Dust centrifuges into a chanced output of Certus Quartz Dust.

quests.title: Glass
quests.subtitle: Glass Hurts, doesn't it...
quests.desc:
Yup - renewable Glass.

Glass'll always be useful throughout your playthrough in a variety of recipes.

quests.title: Diorite
quests.subtitle: Mirabilite, Clay...
quests.desc:
Pulverise your Diorite into Dust, then centrifuge for Mirabilite Dust and Clay Dust.

Mirabilite yields minimal Sulfur, but Clay Dust is the real treasure here. When electrolysed, you'll get some Aluminium for your troubles. Not bad!

quests.title: Granite
quests.subtitle: Redrock, Silicon Dioxide...
quests.desc:
Granite Dust centrifuges into Redrock Dust and the familiar Silicon Dioxide Dust.

Silicon Dioxide is generally just great to have, and Redrock centrifuges into Calcite (that's renewable Calcium) and Flint.

quests.title: Deepslate
quests.subtitle: Silicon Dioxide, Fluorine...
quests.desc:
Deepslate centrifuges into... you guessed it... Silicon Dioxide! You may want to set this up for the guaranteed Biotite output, as Biotite electrolyses into Fluorine.

This is obviously a far better source of Biotite than Stone Dust. Hopefully, you didn't waste your time setting that up!

quests.title: Andesite
quests.subtitle: Asbestos, Saltpeter... (No Create)
quests.desc:
Andesite Dust will get you some delicious Asbestos Dust, and some Saltpeter Dust to boot.

Asbestos is a slow source of Magnesium, and Saltpeter has a variety of uses in chemistry.

quests.title: Obsidian
quests.subtitle: Fallout: New Vegas, Iron...
quests.desc:
By placing some Redstone Dust into your Rock Breaker, you'll find yourself making some Obsidian.

Obsidian Dust is quite useful, as it electrolyses into Iron.

quests.title: Basalt
quests.subtitle: Olivine, Dark Ashes...
quests.desc:
Basalt Dust centrifuges into Olivine, a material which can be used to substitute Emerald in a small handful of recipes - for example, when making Engraved Crystal Chips.

You also get a pittance of Dark Ash.

quests.title: Red Granite
quests.subtitle: Aluminium...
quests.desc:
Not to be confused with regular Granite, Red Granite has some useful utility when the dust is processed. 

Electrolysing it yields Aluminium for slightly fewer steps than the renewable Clay electrolysis.

quests.title: Marble
quests.subtitle: Calcite, Magnesium...
quests.desc: Marble Dust isn't anything to write home about, yielding Calcite and Magnesium.

quests.title: All Kinds Of Air (Distillation)
quests.subtitle: Things are up in the air right now...
quests.desc:
By collecting Air with a Gas Collector and cooling it to Liquid state with a Vacuum Freezer, you can then distill the liquid Air for a plethora of valuable resources.

You thought that was all? Air collection isn't limited to the Overworld - whilst you'll require a higher-tier Distillation Tower to distill the "higher" tiers of liquid Air, you can also process air from the Nether and the End. This process is mostly optional, but you'll definitely find it to be a rewarding one.

quests.title: Liquid Ender Air
quests.subtitle: Nitrogen, Oxygen, Helium, Krypton, Xenon, Radon, Ender Pearls...
quests.desc:
Ender Air is probably the most interesting of the three airs to distill. Requiring an IV Distillation Tower and distilling in batches of 200,000L, you'll need to outfit your DT with at least an IV Input Hatch (or an ME Hatch if you're inclined).

You're getting a ton of Nitrogen, Oxygen, Helium, and Deuterium - the latter of which is commonly used in Fusion. Tritium is used to make Helium Plasma, which is commonly agreed to be the best plasma for power generation.

Krypton and Xenon are your run-of-the-mill EBF-boosting Noble gases. At this point, you should consider running most (if not all) of your EBF recipes on Circuit 2 to benefit from the faster durations.

Would you look at that - you get Radon as well! It's a slow process, especially when you consider that your old setup (did you set it up?) already loops on itself, but more Radon is nice.

Last but not least, you're getting a low chance of Ender Pearl Dust. The chances are low enough that you shouldn't give up on the Mixer recipe just yet... but once again, more Ender Pearls are nice!

quests.title: Liquid Air
quests.subtitle: Nitrogen, Helium, Argon....
quests.desc:
This was touched upon in the EV chapter, but we'll focus on what this distillation gets you here.

The Nitrogen output is substantial, and can help you keep any Pyrolyse Ovens running. You should buffer some of the Nitrogen so you can boost your EBFs - faster recipe times save your sanity and energy in equal measures.

Helium and Argon yields are low, but ideally you can run the Air Distillation as a passive process every so often to keep your supplies topped up. Both are used to boost EBF speeds.

Crushed Ice is... well, Crushed Ice. Maybe you could open a cocktail bar?

quests.title: Liquid Nether Air
quests.subtitle: Carbon, Coal Gas, Helium-3...
quests.desc:
Carbon Monoxide is useful when making Butyraldehyde for your Laminated Glass in late IV.

Coal Gas can be processed in a Distillation Tower for a variety of useful outputs.

Helium-3 is only used in Fusion, and Neon is used to boost a single Blast Furnace recipe - UHV superconductors!

Ashes are always useful for the handful of resources they grant.

quests.title: What about my mob farm?
quests.subtitle: Is it safe? Is it alright?
quests.desc:
Just so you're aware - vanilla farms such as Iron Golems or Piglins have been disabled in this pack. There are obviously ways to cheese the pack (such as some good old Minecraft capitalism, if you catch our drift), but we encourage you to try to play without taking advantage of some notorious vanilla "cheese".

It's possible that in the future, we'll just make a core-mod for the pack, so players can re-enable the disabled mob drops. For now, the drops are disabled via KubeJS script, so if you're script-savvy, the file can be removed at server_scripts/vanilla/loot_drops.js.

quests.title: Ore Generation
quests.subtitle: You'll be an expert by the time you finish reading this!
quests.desc:
Some of the details are repeated from the introduction quest, but we feel like we needed to expand on the explanation if you want to know all the details!

Ready for some "light" reading?

Each vein can contain up to four different ores.

Ore veins generate in a Grid. Every vein is spaced by 3 chunks from one to another in a cardinal direction. Each vein generates in an individual "pattern" - some are round, some are snakey, and others you'll discover for yourself.

They spawn at the Center of the Chunks which have both their X and Z coordinates be at 1 plus a multiple of 3 (-8, -5, -2, 1, 4, 7, ...). You will typically find two veins in the selected chunk - one will be in the Stone layer, and the other vein lays deeper in the Deepslate layer.

However, Ore veins may be deleted by caves, Oil deposits, or from trying to generate above the ground.

It's a good practice to make Waypoints to every vein you find, and to label their type. You may want to come back to it several times.

A classic Ore vein is 7 blocks tall, and ores generate in 4 different patterns:
- The Top Ore generates in the higher 4 layers of the vein.
- The Bottom Ore generates in the lower 3 layers of the vein.
- The Between Ore generates in the middle 3 layers of the vein.
- The Sporadic Ore generates anywhere in the vein. There will be the least amount of this ore in a vein.

The same ore type can be in 2 different generation patterns, such as in Cassiterite veins.

quests.title: Limonite
quests.subtitle: Source of Iron, Copper
quests.desc: A standard Iron vein. It's a good target when you need to replenish your supply.

quests.title: Magnetite
quests.subtitle: Source of Iron, Gold, Vanadium
quests.desc:
This vein is namely one of the few direct sources of Gold in the overworld.

There are many other sources of Gold in GregTech, but you will likely see better yields of Gold if you obtain it by processing other resources.

Vanadium Magnetite becomes more useful in MV, when you will centrifuge out the Vanadium within for Vanadium Steel.

quests.title: Pyrite
quests.subtitle: Source of Copper, Iron
quests.desc:
An excellent Iron-Copper hybrid vein.

Chalcopyrite will play an integral role in the late game, when you're looking to get your hands on some Platinum Group Sludge.

quests.title: Chalcopyrite
quests.subtitle: Source of Copper, Tin
quests.desc:
A great vein for the early game. Copper and Tin to make Bronze, Realgar for your first source of Arsenic, and Zeolite for... nothing much.

Lore: As GregTech used to auto-generate its recipes, Zeolite used to electrolyze in batches of 141 dust at a time. Whoops!

quests.title: Cassiterite
quests.subtitle: #1 Source of Tin
quests.desc: A pure, very rich Tin vein - excellent for early game. You'll be over the moon to find one of these in the Steam Age.

quests.title: Cassiterite Sand
quests.subtitle: Source of Tin
quests.desc:
A decent alternative to the pure Tin vein if you haven't found one yet.

The other ores are as good as decoration.

quests.title: Coal
quests.subtitle: Source of pollution
quests.desc:
As an ore, Coal is hardly worth large-scale processing, because there are too many additional steps. required to turn it into usable fuel. It's still good to have some in your back pocket.

Just run it through a Furnace.

quests.title: Graphite
quests.subtitle: Source of Diamond, Carbon
quests.desc:
Your only source of Diamond!

Diamond has five times more durability than Iron for tools. The catch is that you'll need Diamond Plates for some tools, which is gated behind the Cutting Machine.

Diamond and Graphite both electrolyze into a ton of Carbon. Hint, hint..?

Note: Avoid macerating Diamonds in the early game. Recovering gem dusts into the actual gem cannot be done for a while - and even then, the process is slow and time-consuming...

quests.title: Gamer Rage
quests.subtitle: Source of Salt, Chlorine, Lithium, Fluorine
quests.desc:
Your one-stop-shop for sources of Chlorine.

If you're pining after Lithium, note that it can be obtained from Lepidolite Ore Processing.

quests.title: Olivine
quests.subtitle: Source of Magnesium
quests.desc:
This vein starts to become notable for its Magnesium output starting in the midgame.

Olivine also has various uses, as it can substitute Emerald in a couple of recipes.

quests.title: Garnet
quests.subtitle: Source of Necklaces
quests.desc:
It's shiny! That's about it!

Yeah, you wish you could make jewelry out of it.

quests.title: Apatite
quests.subtitle: Source of Calcium, Phosphorus, Niobium
quests.desc:
Hey, anyone remember Forestry?

This is pretty much the one vein you want to tap in for Phosphorus - which requires quite a few steps of processing to obtain.

Pyrochlore will eventually become extremely valuable, as it's a good source of Niobium.

quests.title: Redstone
quests.subtitle: Source of Redstone, Ruby, Chromium, Mercury
quests.desc:
Your classic Redstone vein.

Ruby is very important for the Chromium it contains. You'll quickly discover that you can never have too much Chromium!

quests.title: Sapphire
quests.subtitle: Source of Aluminium
quests.desc:
Both Sapphire variants serve as your primary source of Aluminium until you can unlock other electrolysis recipes in MV.

The other two gems are mediocre at best.

quests.title: Oilsands
quests.subtitle: Source of the American Dream
quests.desc:
A good source of Heavy Oil, which is useful for Diesel setups early on.

If you don't want to spend time processing Oilsands through your setup, you can always just Centrifuge it directly to get the Heavy Oil.

quests.title: Soapstone
quests.subtitle: Source of Lubricant
quests.desc:
This one's nothing but a meme. It's literally called a Lubricant vein.

With that being said, Lubricant is always useful for your Cutting Machines to keep your factory running smoothly.

quests.title: Lapis
quests.subtitle: Source of Lapis Lazuli, Aluminium, Sodium, Chlorine
quests.desc:
Did you know Lazurite and Sodalite can be used as substitutes for Lapis in enchanting?

Sodalite is the most valuable Ore here, thanks to its useful electrolysis recipe when you get to MV.

quests.title: Pyrolusite
quests.subtitle: Source of Manganese
quests.desc:
Pyrolusite is what most people are here for. It smelts directly into Manganese, which you'll be needing in bulk for HV.

Tantalite will eventually become extremely valuable, thanks to its Niobium byproducts, and its utility when crafting SMD Components.

quests.title: Galena
quests.subtitle: Source of Lead, Silver
quests.desc: The most consistent source of Lead and Silver.

quests.title: Nickel
quests.subtitle: Source of Nickel, Cobalt
quests.desc:
Your one and only trusty source for Nickel.

Cobalt makes for some pretty decent early-game item pipes too!

quests.title: Mica
quests.subtitle: Source of Aluminium, Fluorine, Titanium
quests.desc:
A source of Bauxite for your first ever Titanium.

Kyanite and Mica decompose into some desirable products if you electrolyze them, but there are definitely better options, so you shouldn't hunt them down if you want their byproducts.

(This vein probably irks your average GTNH player...)

quests.title: Mineral Sand
quests.subtitle: Source of Iron
quests.desc:
The worst Iron vein. It's helpful in the early game, but that's about it.

We love you anyway, Mineral Sand vein!

quests.title: Bastnatite
quests.subtitle: Source of Rare Earth, Neodymium, Fluorine
quests.desc:
The de-facto Rare Earth vein for progression in EV... and onwards.

Lore: This vein was bugged for quite some time. It's never actually contained any Monazite, and nobody realised (well, nobody brought it up!) for many, many months. Did you notice?

quests.title: Emerald
quests.subtitle: Source of Beryllium, Emerald, Thorium
quests.desc: Emerald is the useful ore here. It's used in small quantities in MV, and is a part of the Crystal process for Crystal and Wetware circuits much later on.

quests.title: Molybdenum
quests.subtitle: Source of Molybdenum
quests.desc:
This vein is very rare! Congratulations on stumbling upon one.

(Or at least... it should be rare.)

View the contents of this vein as "basically Molybdenum, and some other stuff". Yeah, Molybdenum is what's important here.

quests.title: Certus Quartz
quests.subtitle: Source of AE2, Barium
quests.desc:
Your ticket into Applied Energistics 2 is nestled deep within a Certus Quartz vein.

Alongside this, note that Barite electrolyses into Barium, which you will eventually need to make some higher tier Superconductors.

quests.title: Saltpeter
quests.subtitle: Source of Potassium, Electrum
quests.desc:
Electrotine is important for progression in HV. It centrifuges into Electrum (a source of Gold and Silver), and Redstone.

Saltpeter is used in a few reactions, and... the other two ores here are pretty average.

Lore: Does the word "Nikolite" ring a bell? It's pretty much Electrotine's great-ancestor, hailing from RedPower.

quests.title: Topaz
quests.subtitle: Source of Copper, Fluorine
quests.desc:
Both Topaz ores are excellent subjects for eventual electrolysis.

This makes this vein one of the best Fluorine sources.

quests.title: Tetrahedrite
quests.subtitle: Source of Copper, Antimony
quests.desc:
Almost a pure Copper vein. Damn you, Stibnite, ruining our purity!

You can hardly complain about its presence, though - Stibnite smelts directly into Antimony, which you will want in LV for your Batteries.

quests.title: Limonite
quests.subtitle: Source of Iron, Gold
quests.desc:
This Nether Limonite vein variant isn't to be slept on.

Thanks to the doubled yield of Nether ores, this is an excellent source of Iron and Gold.

quests.title: Sulfur
quests.subtitle: Source of Sulfur, Iron, Zinc
quests.desc:
Your first Nether expedition should be for Sulfur - which is found in this vein! Be careful, as this vein usually spawns from y-levels of 10 to 30. Bring Fire Resistance potions!

Sphalerite is extremely valuable, as its ore processing gives Gallium. It also smelts directly into Zinc.

quests.title: Quartz
quests.subtitle: Source of Nether Quartz, Silicon
quests.desc:
Your classic Nether Quartz vein.

Silicon isn't too difficult to come by, but Quartzite isn't a bad source.

quests.title: Magnetite
quests.subtitle: Source of Iron, Vanadium, Gold, Chromium
quests.desc:
You may already have boatloads of Iron by the time you make it here, but don't overlook the utility of this vein.

Chromite is an excellent source of Chromium, and Vanadium is needed in higher quantities in the late game.

quests.title: Naquadah
quests.subtitle: Source of... a lot.
quests.desc:
Seasoned sci-fi fans might recognise Naquadah from the series Stargate! You'll find this becomes an essential material from LuV onwards.

Naquadah can be processed in LuV for Titanium, Trinium, Barium, Gallium, Indium, Phosphorus, Enriched Naquadah, and Naquadria. Versatile!

Moving the spotlight to the other ore, Plutonium is used to start the Radon loop, required in IV.

quests.title: Sheldonite
quests.subtitle: Source of a lot, again...
quests.desc:
Platinum starts seeing usage in EV.

The Platinum Group Sludge chain becomes a requirement in IV, and Sheldonite is the best ore to kickstart your processing.

Lore: The real-world name for Sheldonite ore is actually Cooperite. GregoriusT thought it was "haha, funny" to make it a reference to Sheldon Cooper, despite not being the person the ore was named after!  We kept the "haha, funny" joke going.

quests.title: Scheelite
quests.subtitle: Source of Tungsten, Lithium
quests.desc:
Your one and only Tungsten source! Whilst you could technically get your Tungstate from macerating End Stone, the yield is tiny. Process these ores instead!

An extremely important vein, as Tungsten is used in vast amounts in IV and onwards.

quests.title: Bauxite
quests.subtitle: Source of Aluminium, Titanium, Gallium
quests.desc:
The vein to mine for Aluminium and Titanium if you're a well-adjusted person.

Ditch those Mica veins - Bauxite is the future!

quests.title: Pitchblende
quests.subtitle: Source of Uranium, Thorium, Lead
quests.desc:
The one and only vein for Uranium!

Uranium has a few minor uses in EV and beyond.

Did you know that Nuclear Reactors might be making their way to GTCEu in the future?

quests.title: The First Maceration
quests.subtitle: Well. You've gotta start somewhere!
quests.desc:
All good ore processing lines start with a crush, and in your case, you'll want to macerate either your Raw Ore, or your Ore blocks (if you're using Silk Touch!)

When you can make the large multiblock Miners, your ore yield is almost quadrupled further, and you won't need to macerate for the first step - the Large Miner will output its ores as if they had been macerated.

quests.title: Washing with Distilled Water
quests.subtitle: The "distinguished" version of water
quests.desc:
Nothing special here. Any ores that you don't wash in novelty chemicals simply need to be washed in water with an Ore Washer. You should use Distilled Water, as it cuts the recipe time down by 50%. Set up a couple of Distilleries on-site and buffer into an appropriately-sized Super Tank.

If you don't care about the byproducts in this section, you can wash the ores in regular water without a configured circuit to process 25x faster, but without byproducts.

quests.title: Washing with Sodium Persulfate
quests.subtitle: Sulfium Persodate
quests.desc:
Sodium Persulfate isn't as simple to obtain as Mercury, but you'll probably find yourself making some when crafting Circuit Boards. Set up a Chemical Reactor/Electrolyser combo with a Level emitter attached if you want to keep some in stock, and then you can wash any Crushed Ore with a Chemical Bath.

You'll want to use Sodium Persulfate when processing:

- Tin Ore
- Aluminium Ore
- Cobalt Ore
- Cobaltite Ore
- Bauxite Ore
- Tetrahedrite Ore
- Pentlandite Ore
- Sphalerite Ore
- Stibnite Ore
- Yellow Limonite Ore
- Malachite Ore
- Iron Ore

Enjoy your byproducts!

quests.title: Washing with Mercury
quests.subtitle: Bathing with Venus
quests.desc:
Washing ores with Mercury where applicable instead of Distilled Water grants unique byproducts that may be difficult to obtain in other circumstances. It's usually worth doing this, as Mercury is readily available from centrifuging Redstone or Cinnabar (and in fact, Mercury is the only real usage of Cinnabar!).

Simply wash any Crushed Ore with a Chemical Bath.

Here is a non-exhaustive list of ores you likely want to wash in Mercury:

- Galena Ore
- Tungstate Ore
- Silver Ore
- Bornite Ore
- Magnetite Ore
- Copper Ore
- Gold Ore
- Nickel Ore
- Platinum Ore
- Chalcopyrite Ore
- Sheldonite Ore

These alternate "washing routes" will increase your Gold, Silver, and Platinum Group yields significantly!

quests.title: Thermal Centrifuge
quests.subtitle: This isn't Thermal Expansion...
quests.desc:
The default way to deal with ores at this step is usually with the Thermal Centrifuge for its exclusive byproduct, but check around the above quest to make sure you're not missing something important.

Allez!

quests.title: We've got some Purified Ore - what's next?
quests.subtitle: Started from the Raw Ore, now we here
quests.desc: From now onwards, you'll be sending all of your ores towards all kinds of places, depending on how far you are through your playthrough. Quests will unlock around this node when you unlock the ability to deal with them, so check back occasionally if you're feeling bored.

quests.title: We did it! Now what?
quests.subtitle: That wasn't so bad, now.
quests.desc:
From now? That's the basics of an ore processing setup. You can check your filters, make more machines, and generally improve efficiency, but if you've loosely followed everything on this page, you should be in good stead!

Hopefully, some information here was of use.

quests.title: Slurry Processing
quests.subtitle: Not quite like Mekanism
quests.desc:
Some Gem ores - namely Ruby, Sapphire, and Green Sapphire - can be mixed with Aqua Regia in their crushed form to obtain Slurry.

When electrolysed, the Slurry has some interesting byproducts. Namely, it's one of the few ways to obtain Titanium Dust without macerating Titanium ingots!

quests.title: Electrolyse what's left!
quests.subtitle: We don't need THIS much Cobaltite, right?
quests.desc: Some dusts in GregTech have little use other than to be processed in an Electrolyser for some nice bonus chemical outputs. You can either set a manual filter for the desired items in a Storage Bus, or provide the most vital dusts via ME Interface, for example.

quests.title: Do we need to package?
quests.subtitle: With our powers combined...
quests.desc:
Some stages listed on this page output Small/Tiny Dusts, or Nuggets. These have little use in such a form, so it's never a bad idea to route these into a Packer to "compress" them into their full forms.

If you're having problems with the Packer filling up with too few Nuggets and clogging your buffer, then you may want to consider using a GregTech Robot Arm set to "Supply Exact". Send your Nuggets in 9 at a time, and you can consider this a problem solved!

quests.title: Sifting
quests.subtitle: Great Grey Wolf Sif-ting
quests.desc: Gemstones deserve to be sifted. In earlier stages of the game, this is slow and has poor yields, but when overclocked, sifting quickly becomes a great way to maximise your gemstone yield.

quests.title: Indium Processing
quests.subtitle: Getting your first Indium has never been so fun.
quests.desc:
As mentioned in the main sections of this quest book, this Indium setup has a limited lifetime, so don't stress over optimising every last detail.

Simply mix Purified Galena and Sphalerite together with some Sulfuric Acid to obtain Indium Concentrate, then combine with Aluminium Dust in a Chemical Reactor for your prize!

quests.title: Platinum Group Processing
quests.subtitle: Quite the rabbit hole to jump down...
quests.desc:
You'll want to send off the following ores to Platinum Group Processing:

- Purified Chalcopyrite Ore
- Purified Tetrahedrite Ore
- Purified Chalcocite Ore
- Purified Bornite Ore
- Purified Sheldonite Ore
- Purified Pentlandite Ore

Treat these ores with Nitric Acid to receive your Platinum Group sludge, alongside a Sulfuric byproduct fluid. What you do with these outputs... is up to you to figure out!

(Hint: It's in the EV chapter.)

quests.title: Electromagnetic Separator
quests.subtitle: Maximising Naquadah
quests.desc:
The Electromagnetic Separator is an interesting machine that skips two processing stages, but usually returns unique byproducts that aren't usually too simple to obtain. You'll need to process down pure dusts to use this - so make sure you route any desired ores correctly.

You may appreciate performing this stage of processing on the following ores:

- Bastnasite Ore (This is particularly strong for Neodymium!)
- Naquadah Ore (The Enriched Naquadah byproduct is very powerful!)
- Nickel Ore
- Tin Ore
- Goethite Ore
- Hematite Ore
- Chromite Ore
- Ilmenite Ore
- Pyrite Ore
- Yellow Limonite Ore
- Glauconite Sand Ore
- Magnetite Ore
- Vanadium Magnetite Ore
- Granitic Mineral Sand Ore
- Basaltic Mineral Sand Ore

quests.title: Start here!
quests.subtitle: From one Ore, a great many dusts shall flow...
quests.desc:
Hello! You're probably looking to set up basic ore processing. This quest line will try to guide you through the necessary motions. As always, it's important to make the disclaimer that this isn't the only "meta" way to process your ores - a strong ore processing facility will treat each ore depending on the demands of your GregTech factory. We emphasise that you shouldn't take this guide as gospel.

Ready to get into the weeds?

quests.title: Oh no! Pure dust?!
quests.subtitle: Fear not! We have our solution!
quests.desc: A side product of sifting is the production of pure dusts. These shouldn't be sent back to your main storage, because they can't be used in recipes... and centrifuging them will give you a sweet, sweet extra byproduct.

quests.title: How would I boost my processing power?
quests.desc: Multiple on-tier machines are never a bad idea, but you should definitely consider using the Large multiblock variants of the singleblock machines from Gregicality Multiblocks. They're significantly easier to upgrade as you progress through the later tiers of the mod (would you rather make a 16A LuV Energy Hatch, or 16 LuV Macerators?!), and plus... they look rad as hell.

quests.title: Applied Energistics 2
quests.subtitle: Let's look at what AE2 can do for you!
quests.desc: Whilst the majority (if not all) of the listed steps can be automated using GregTech alone, savvy players may wish to use AE2 for all their logistics needs. Here, we'll cover some methods you may not be aware of to help you elevate your Ore Processing to the next level.

quests.title: Subnets 101
quests.subtitle: Socially Distanced Network
quests.desc:
One could define a Subnetwork (or "Subnet" for short) as any network that isn't part of your main network.

The simplest way to create a Subnet is by attaching an ME Interface to a Storage Bus. This means that the Storage Bus will treat the Interface as an inventory to be read from - i.e., it'll be able to see all of the items in the network connected to the Interface.

As your main network will not be transferred through the Storage Bus, you'll need to give your subnet power from the main network with Quartz Fibre. Ensure that you don't accidentally connect the two networks with regular cabling.

Question is - how does this benefit you as a player? The logistics powers made available with subnetworking are immense, so we'll cover a few examples in light detail. The rest is left as an exercise to the reader!

- A subnet for your ore processing system, which automatically handles all the intermediate byproducts without them constantly entering and exiting your main network!
- Really, it's useful to subnet larger processing chains such as Platinum processing. This way, you can ensure that byproducts can be appropriately recycled, and reduce overhead.



Subnets can be infinitely nested, but as always in modded Minecraft, this should be performed in moderation (or even better, avoided), as many levels of nesting can cause performance issues when calculating the network.

Oh... and if you've re-enabled Channels in this pack, subnetworks only consume a single channel from your main network. What a bargain!

quests.title: Craftable in Steam Age and LV
quests.subtitle: Hand crafted and artisanal
quests.desc:
Early game Circuits are hand craftable.

Pick up the Circuit Assembler to make them a lot more efficient.

quests.title: Craftable in MV
quests.subtitle: Integrated Dynamics
quests.desc:
The Integrated Circuit line may not be a strict upgrade in terms of cost, but will tide you over as a stepping stone towards the next tier.

They will require a MV Laser Engraver and a MV Cutting Machine.

Lore: In Omnifactory, and in previous Gregicality, this circuit line was called Refined and was standardized like further tiers.

quests.title: Craftable in MV and HV
quests.subtitle: Micro Miners
quests.desc:
Micro Circuits are where the Cleanroom becomes a requirement to progress.

Don't get caught out by the Mainframe - it requires a HV Circuit Assembler.

quests.title: Craftable in EV
quests.subtitle: Nano Age
quests.desc:
This is the Nano Circuit line.

Starting from here, circuit progression will follow a pattern:

- Processor
- Assembly
- Supercomputer
- Mainframe

quests.title: Craftable in IV
quests.subtitle: GregTech gets Quantum
quests.desc:
Welcome to the Quantum Circuit line.

You may want to invest in PBI at this point, as Advanced SMDs will skyrocket your circuit production.

quests.title: Craftable in LuV
quests.subtitle: Crystallisation
quests.desc:
This is the Crystal Circuit line.

It's locked behind Fusion.

quests.title: Craftable in ZPM
quests.subtitle: Soft and Wetware
quests.desc:
This is the Wetware Circuit line.

It will be the biggest automation challenge to face in this modpack. Best of luck.

quests.title: ULV Circuits
quests.desc: On this line you can see the Steam, or ULV, equivalents.

quests.title: LV Circuits
quests.desc:
Circuits along this line are LV, and are interchangeable in recipes.

Electronic Circuits are the "worst" in terms of cost and effort required to produce, but you have to start somewhere.

Basic Integrated Processors act as a middle layer between Electronic Circuits and Microchip Processors.

Finally, Microchip Processors will be the best option for the rest of the playthrough.

quests.title: MV Circuits
quests.desc:
Circuits along this line are MV, and are interchangeable in recipes.

The pattern stays the same: the rightmost circuit comes in larger quantities and is overall easier to produce.

quests.title: HV Circuits
quests.desc:
Circuits along this line are HV, and are interchangeable in recipes.

The pattern stays the same: the rightmost circuit comes in larger quantities and is overall easier to produce.

quests.title: EV Circuits
quests.desc:
Circuits along this line are EV, and are interchangeable in recipes.

The pattern stays the same: the rightmost circuit comes in larger quantities and is overall easier to produce.

quests.title: IV Circuits
quests.desc:
Circuits along this line are IV, and are interchangeable in recipes.

The pattern stays the same: the rightmost circuit comes in larger quantities and is overall easier to produce.

quests.title: LuV Circuits
quests.desc:
Circuits along this line are LuV, and are interchangeable in recipes.

The pattern stays the same: the rightmost circuit comes in larger quantities and is overall easier to produce.

Make sure to set up wetware board production as soon as possible! These take a very long time to make.

It's LuV, not LUV.

quests.title: ZPM Circuits
quests.desc: Circuits along this line are ZPM, and are interchangeable in recipes.

quests.title: UV Circuits
quests.desc: Circuits along this line are UV, and are interchangeable for each other.

quests.title: UHV Circuits
quests.desc:
Congratulations! You've reached the end of the circuit progression.

The only UHV circuit is the Wetware Mainframe, which is used for crafting some endgame stuff.

quests.title: Cupronickel Ingot
quests.subtitle: Legally distinct Constantan
quests.desc:
Gotta say, Cupronickel ingots look pretty tasty.

Lore: IRL, Cupronickel usually contains additional elements such as Iron or Manganese to improve its strength. The Cupronickel in GregTech is probably more like Constantan (55:45 Copper:Nickel).

quests.title: Kanthal Ingot
quests.subtitle: That's just how I FeAlCr
quests.desc:
Lore: You might find Kanthal around the home. Due to its excellent thermal conductivity and intermediate electric resistance, Kanthal is usually found in toasters and heaters!

Bet you didn't expect your toaster to be at MV before you!

quests.title: Nichrome
quests.subtitle: Operation Nichrome Dome
quests.desc: Nichrome will require your Electric Blast Furnace to be at least EV, with Kanthal coils to boot.
